title: Europe's missile defence gap: is South Korea part of the answer?
gist: Europe faces a structural shortfall in medium- and long-range ballistic-missile defence as US inventory depletion and political unreliability reduce American supply capacity. South Korea, now the second-largest arms supplier to European NATO members, has an opening to fill this gap — particularly with its Cheongung-II system that achieved 96%+ intercept rates in 2026. EU industrial-policy rules (SAFE initiative's 65% local-content requirement) are the primary structural barrier to South Korean penetration of this market.

insights.claim: US strikes on Iran in June 2025 and Operation Epic Fury in February 2026 consumed 20% of Washington's SM-3 interceptors and 20–50% of THAAD missiles, creating a structural depletion that takes years to replenish at current production rates.
insights.speaker: Lami Kim
insights.so_what: US inventory depletion is not a political choice but a physical constraint — near-term export capacity to Europe is severely limited regardless of administration policy, validating demand for non-US missile defence suppliers.
insights.quote: Up to 20% of Washington's SM-3 interceptors and 20–50% of Lockheed Martin Terminal High Altitude Area Defense (THAAD) missiles were expended by the US in the June 2025 conflict involving Iran and Israel.

insights.claim: Lockheed Martin produced only 620 Patriot interceptors in 2025, while Gulf countries alone expended approximately 800 Patriot interceptors during the 2026 Iran conflict, making replacement timelines multi-year — Europe will be last in the queue.
insights.speaker: Lami Kim
insights.so_what: The production-consumption mismatch is quantifiable: a single regional conflict consumed 1.3 years of Patriot production in one engagement. Any European government budgeting for Patriot systems should price in 3–5 year delivery delays, boosting the investment case for alternative suppliers.
insights.quote: Lockheed Martin produced 620 Patriot missile interceptors in 2025. The resulting depletion in inventory holdings prompted the US to redeploy Patriot systems from South Korea to the Middle East in early 2026.

insights.claim: The US redeployed Patriot systems from South Korea to the Middle East in early 2026, signalling that even treaty allies in the Indo-Pacific are being stripped of assets — Europe's tier in the US priority order is below South Korea.
insights.speaker: Lami Kim
insights.so_what: If South Korea — a frontline ally with an active nuclear threat — is being stripped of US assets, European planners and defence investors should treat US supply as structurally unavailable for the next 2–4 years, not merely politically uncertain.

themes.theme: South Korea as Europe's alternative missile defence supplier

insights.claim: South Korea became the second-largest supplier of major arms to European NATO members in 2021–25, behind only the US, demonstrating logistics, delivery reliability, and political compatibility that positions it for further market share in missile defence.
insights.speaker: Lami Kim
insights.so_what: South Korea's European arms market share is already structural, not marginal. Investors in Korean defence primes (Hanwha, LIG Nex1) should price in continuation of a multi-year, multi-country export cycle, not a one-off Poland deal.
insights.quote: According to a 2026 report by the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute, South Korea was the second-largest supplier of major arms to European NATO members during the 2021–25 period, behind only the US.

insights.claim: The Cheongung-II system achieved an interception success rate exceeding 96% in the 2026 Iran conflict after UAE deployment, generating emergency follow-on orders from Saudi Arabia and Iraq — combat-proven performance data now available for European procurement evaluations.
insights.speaker: Lami Kim
insights.so_what: Combat validation of the Cheongung-II in a high-density threat environment removes the primary procurement risk for European buyers. A 96%+ intercept rate in live operations is a stronger sales argument than any test data, and will accelerate European evaluation timelines.
insights.quote: Two systems were deployed in early 2026, shortly before the outbreak of the Iran conflict, and reportedly achieved an interception success rate exceeding 96%, drawing significant international attention.

insights.claim: South Korean systems are approximately 95% domestically produced, reducing supply-chain exposure for buyers and limiting US extraterritorial export-control leverage — a structural advantage over US and jointly-developed systems containing American components.
insights.speaker: Lami Kim
insights.so_what: European buyers increasingly concerned about US reliability as a guarantor will value South Korean systems' independence from US re-export approval. This 95% domestic content figure is a key differentiator from Patriot (US), NASAMS (Norway/US), and Arrow 3 (Israel/Boeing).
insights.quote: One advantage of South Korea's systems arguably lies in their high degree of domestic production – estimated at around 95% – which reduces vulnerability to supply-chain disruptions and export restrictions while helping to keep production costs low.

insights.claim: South Korea's L-SAM upper-tier system, analogous to THAAD, is expected to be deployment-ready by 2028, positioning Korean defence firms to eventually offer a layered intercept stack competitive with the full US system suite.
insights.speaker: Lami Kim
insights.so_what: L-SAM's 2028 readiness date creates a near-term investment catalyst: Korean defence primes will need to ramp production capacity and international certification processes in 2026–27 in anticipation of export demand. Watch for capex announcements and European government letters of intent.
insights.quote: South Korea's L-SAM system – sometimes described as a Korean equivalent to THAAD – could further enhance its appeal as a supplier of higher-tier missile-defence capabilities once the system is fully ready for deployment, expected by 2028.

themes.theme: EU industrial policy as a market-shaping constraint

insights.claim: The EU's SAFE initiative (€150bn, adopted May 2025) requires 65% EU-origin content for loan eligibility, structurally excluding South Korean and other non-EU systems from the bulk of European defence funding — creating a two-tier procurement market.
insights.speaker: Lami Kim
insights.so_what: SAFE's content rules create a regulatory moat for EU defence primes (MBDA, Thales, Leonardo, Rheinmetall) on EU-funded contracts while opening a separate lane for South Korean systems in national budgets outside SAFE. Investors should distinguish between SAFE-eligible and SAFE-excluded defence spend.
insights.quote: To qualify for a loan under this initiative, projects must meet a requirement that at least 65% of the product is produced within the EU, effectively prioritising domestic production. South Korea's exclusion from SAFE limits the attractiveness of its systems for EU-funded procurement.

insights.claim: Hanwha's expansion of manufacturing facilities in Poland and Romania — though not yet for missile interceptors — creates a potential path to EU local-content compliance that could eventually qualify South Korean systems for SAFE funding.
insights.speaker: Lami Kim
insights.so_what: Hanwha's European manufacturing footprint is the strategic variable to watch: if it expands into interceptor production in Poland or Romania, South Korean systems could become SAFE-eligible and access the full €150bn pool. This is a medium-term optionality that the market may not be pricing into Hanwha's valuation.
insights.quote: Hanwha is expanding production in Europe, including building new facilities in Poland and Romania. Although these plants are not intended for missile-interceptor production, they could potentially be adapted for that purpose.

themes.theme: Europe's strategic autonomy dilemma and defence spending durability

insights.claim: European governments have spent approximately $50bn on air and missile defence since 2022, primarily on US systems, yet remain acutely short of medium- and long-range ballistic-missile defence — indicating that spending velocity exceeds industrial output capacity industry-wide.
insights.speaker: Lami Kim
insights.so_what: The $50bn spend with still-acute gaps confirms that European defence budgets are supply-constrained, not demand-constrained. This is structurally bullish for any certified missile-defence supplier that can actually deliver — pricing power and order backlogs should remain elevated for years.
insights.quote: European governments have spent heavily – reportedly around US$50 billion since 2022 – primarily on US systems.

insights.claim: Europe faces a genuine structural dilemma: short-term readiness requires importing non-EU systems, while long-term autonomy requires building EU industrial capacity — these goals compete for the same defence euros and political attention simultaneously.
insights.speaker: Lami Kim
insights.so_what: This dilemma will not resolve quickly — expect European procurement to bifurcate into urgent national-budget purchases from Korea/Israel and longer-cycle EU-funded development of domestic systems. Companies serving both channels (e.g., MBDA, Thales) are better positioned than pure-play importers.
insights.quote: Europe faces a dilemma. On the one hand, it must rebuild and strengthen its own defence-industrial base to ensure long-term autonomy. On the other hand, it must address immediate capability gaps in missile defence.

insights.claim: The SAMP/T (Eurosam) system — Europe's only indigenous ballistic-missile defence alternative to Patriot — has far fewer sales and constrained production capacity, meaning European industrial autonomy in this segment is years away despite heavy investment.
insights.speaker: Lami Kim
insights.so_what: The political narrative of European defence autonomy outpaces industrial reality in missile defence specifically. This gap sustains demand for non-EU suppliers for longer than political rhetoric implies, and creates risk of over-investment in European primes that cannot scale interceptor production quickly.
insights.quote: Europe has an alternative to Patriot – the SAMP/T surface-to-air missile system, developed by the Franco-Italian Eurosam consortium (MBDA and Thales). The system, however, has secured far fewer sales than its US competitor, and its production capacity remains constrained.
